metadata.summary: Phase-by-phase execution playbook for org-scale PM tool migrations: six phases (Planning through Stabilization) with ETL engine, cutover checklist, rollback strategy, and change-management comms plan.

content.title: Cross-Tool PM Migration — Process
content.summary: A phase-by-phase execution playbook for migrating project data between PM tools at org scale: six phases (Planning → Preparation → Pilot → Full Migration → Cutover → Stabilization) with an ETL engine, cutover checklist, rollback strategy, and change-management comms plan. Designed for migrations touching ≥3 teams, ≥1k issues, or any compliance scope.

why.p: Large PM migrations fail most often at two points: data transformation (field mismatches surface only in production) and cutover (rate limits, missing ID mappings, and silent attachment loss). A phase-gated playbook with dry-run-first ETL and rehearsed rollback converts unpredictable cutovers into repeatable operations with clear abort criteria.

Phase durations from guidelines are guidance, not law; novel custom-field setups blow up the Preparation phase. Cutover windows assume linear API access; rate limits routinely double the planned downtime. Stabilization always overruns; budget 2× the planned support window. Rollback only works if no critical data was created in the target during cutover — a single shipped feature with target-only context can pin you. Mid-migration "small scope additions" silently double the work; freeze the mapping spec at the end of Preparation.

list.item: You have completed field-mapping and audit (see tool-migration-basics) and need an execution playbook.
Org-wide migration touching ≥3 teams, ≥1k issues, or a compliance scope.
Multi-wave migrations where teams cut over in batches across weeks.
Cutover windows requiring rollback rehearsal and freeze coordination.
Post-merger consolidation requiring two source tools → one target.
Single-team or <100 issue migration — use basics + a one-day cutover; this playbook adds overhead.
Vendor-managed migration where their tooling does extract/transform/load — defer to it.
Migrations during an active product launch or incident — wait for a quieter window.
Projects where the primary risk is political (team adoption), not technical — invest in change management depth instead.

section.name: six-migration-phases
section.p: Execute all six phases in order; skipping Preparation or Pilot is the leading cause of data-loss incidents at cutover. Phase 3 (Pilot) surfaces field-mapping failures on a small dataset at low cost; skipping it causes those failures to appear at Phase 4 scale with no rollback window.

rule.statement: Complete Phase 1 Planning with explicit stakeholder alignment, tool selection or evaluation, scope definition, timeline with 50% buffer over measured dry-run time, and risk assessment.
rule.rationale: Skipping planning or condensing it into a few days causes scope creep, misaligned expectations, and timeline overruns that compound through subsequent phases.

list.item: Stakeholder alignment and sponsorship
Tool selection or evaluation (see pm-tools-comparison)
Migration scope definition — in-scope projects, date range, attachments threshold
Timeline creation with explicit buffer (50% over measured dry-run time)
Risk assessment: data loss, integration failure, adoption resistance

rule.statement: Freeze the field mapping specification at end of Preparation; mid-migration mapping changes silently double scope.
rule.rationale: Each mapping change requires re-transforming and re-validating already-migrated records.

list.item: Target tool setup and admin configuration
Field mapping design — document every source field to target field, with fallback for unmapped
Migration script development (ETL engine)
Test environment creation for dry-run
Team communication: announce timeline, schedule training

antipattern.description: Picking the simplest team for the pilot project.
antipattern.reason: Creates false confidence; the simplest team has the fewest edge cases and the most forgiving stakeholders. Failures surface in Phase 4 at scale instead.

list.item: Select pilot project — representative complexity, NOT the simplest team
Execute migration with dry-run flag first, then live
Validate issue count, comment count, attachment byte count
Sample 50 items by hand before declaring pilot success
Gather feedback, adjust approach
Execute in waves by team or project, not by record type
Validate each wave (three numbers: issues, comments, attachments-by-byte)
Address issues between waves; do not accumulate debt
Monitor rate limits; add 50% buffer to planned wave duration

rule.statement: Keep the source tool read-only for at least 30 days post-cutover; never delete it during stabilization.
rule.rationale: Teams reference historical context from source; premature deletion forces re-migration of data that was intentionally excluded.

list.item: T-24h: final export from source, backup target, notify all users
T-4h: set source to read-only, run final migration sync, validate counts
T-0: enable target for all users, send go-live announcement, support team on standby
T+1h: verify login success, automations running, address immediate issues
T+24h: review error logs, collect feedback, document known issues
T+1w: adoption metrics, complete missing migrations, retrospective, plan source decommission

rule.statement: Budget 2x the planned stabilization window; it always overruns.
rule.rationale: Edge cases and user adaptation issues accumulate in week 3-4 after initial excitement fades.

list.item: Active support channel with named owner
Fix issues within 24 hours of report
Run training sessions for high-friction groups
Document lessons learned; schedule source decommission on a calendar date

section.name: etl-migration-engine
section.p: The ETL engine is the core of Phase 3–4 execution. Design it as a CLI tool that agents invoke, not as inline LLM calls. Long-running migrations exceed agent token budgets; the CLI pattern keeps the agent as orchestrator with human-reviewable artifacts at each checkpoint.

rule.statement: Design the migration engine as a CLI tool the agent invokes; do not run migrations as inline LLM calls.
rule.rationale: Long-running migrations exceed agent token budgets; the CLI pattern keeps the agent as orchestrator with human-reviewable artifacts at each checkpoint.

rule.statement: Run a full dry-run on production data into a sandbox target one week before cutover; measure exact runtime then add 50% buffer.
rule.rationale: Rate limits routinely double planned cutover downtime; dry-run measurement is the only reliable source of timing data.

example.title: MigrationConfig dataclass
example.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import Dict
@dataclass class MigrationConfig: source_tool: str target_tool: str field_mapping: Dict[str, str] # source_field -> target_field status_mapping: Dict[str, str] # source_status -> target_status priority_mapping: Dict[str, str] dry_run: bool = True # always default to dry_run=True
example.note: Always default dry_run to True to prevent accidental live migrations.

rule.statement: Freeze the field mapping specification before the first dry-run; forbid runtime mapping mutations.
rule.rationale: Each mapping change requires re-transforming already-processed records; LLMs that "fix" mappings on the fly silently corrupt the ID mapping and cause orphaned links.

list.item: Extract: pull all issues from source; log count before processing
Transform: apply field_mapping, status_mapping, priority_mapping per record; log per-record errors without halting
Load: create issues in target; record source_id → target_id mapping; checkpoint after each successful write
Validate: compare three counts (issues, comments, attachments-by-byte) and sample 50 random items by hand

section.name: checkpointed-loader
section.p: The checkpoint file enables resume from any point; critical for multi-hour migrations interrupted by rate limits.

example.title: Checkpointed loader script

section.name: post-migration-link-fixing
section.p: Issue links, @mentions in comments, and commit message references all contain source-tool IDs. Fix these in a separate pass after the main migration using the ID mapping JSON.

rule.statement: Force-list the 0.5% of failed records; they are always the most-linked tickets.
rule.rationale: A 99.5% success rate looks fine in aggregate but the missing records are disproportionately high-value due to cross-reference density.

antipattern.description: Skipping @mention and user-ID resolution during comment migration.
antipattern.reason: Strips all @mentions silently, breaking notification chains and audit trails for team collaboration history.

section.name: cutover-and-rollback
section.p: Cutover execution and rollback strategy determine whether migrations succeed or catastrophically fail.

rule.statement: Validate three numbers before declaring any wave or cutover successful: issue count, comment count, and attachment count by byte.
rule.rationale: Issue count alone misses corrupted comments and missing attachments; the three-number check catches the most common silent data-loss patterns.

rule.statement: Cutover on a low-traffic window (Friday afternoon if org tolerates weekend quiet); never mid-week for large migrations.
rule.rationale: Weekend windows provide recovery time before full business-hours load resumes; mid-week cutovers interrupt peak productivity with no buffer.

list.item: T-24h: final data export from source, backup target environment, notify all users of timeline, prepare support team
T-4h: set source tool to read-only, run final migration sync, validate issue counts, test critical integrations
T-0: enable target tool for all users, send go-live announcement, support team on standby, monitor error rate
T+1h: verify user login success, confirm automations running, address immediate issues, update stakeholders
T+24h: review error logs, collect user feedback, document known issues, plan remediation
T+1w: measure adoption metrics, complete any missing migrations, conduct retrospective, plan source tool decommission date

antipattern.description: Agent marks checklist steps complete based on stdout text matches rather than process exit codes.
antipattern.reason: Text matches lie; a process can print "success" while returning exit code 1. Use exit codes for machine-verified steps.

rule.statement: Pre-write rollback communications before cutover begins so they can be sent within seconds of a decision to revert.
rule.rationale: Rollback decisions under stress are slowed by the need to draft communications; pre-writing removes this bottleneck and enables faster response.

list.item: Data loss detected → immediate rollback
Critical integration failure → immediate rollback
Team productivity drop ≥50% → rollback within 48 hours
User adoption under 30% at end of week 1 → rollback within 1 week
Pause migration — halt all ETL processes
Notify stakeholders immediately with pre-written message
Assess damage scope — what data was created only in target during cutover?
Restore source read access
Document failure root cause
Export any target-only data before reverting

rule.statement: Rollback only works if no critical data was created exclusively in the target during cutover; a single shipped feature with target-only context can pin you.
rule.rationale: Once teams start creating tickets in the new tool, those records don't exist in the source; rollback would lose that work, making true revert impossible.

list.item: T-4 weeks: migration announcement to all users via email + Slack + meeting
T-2 weeks: training schedule distributed via email + calendar invites
T-1 week: final preparation checklist via email + Slack
T-0: go-live announcement via email + Slack + meeting for high-impact teams
Daily for 2 weeks post-cutover: tips, FAQs, and support channel reminder via Slack

rule.statement: Rate-limit webhook-based notifications to one summary per migration phase; do not send a message per event.
rule.rationale: Retry storms in migration engines can trigger hundreds of webhook notifications, spamming channels and causing teams to mute the notifications needed for real alerts.

section.name: agentic-workflows
section.p: When using agents or subagents to assist multi-phase migrations, watch for these failure modes.

list.item: migration-planner — produces the project plan Markdown from scope inputs, risk register, comms calendar.
migration-engine — runs Extract → Transform → Load with dry-run flag, checkpoint files, ID mapping JSON.
cutover-runner — executes the checklist top-to-bottom, halts on validation failure, posts status updates.
link-fixer — runs after main migration; updates issue links in commit messages, wikis, Slack archives via search/replace bots.
migration-retro — collects user-feedback survey + support tickets, produces lessons-learned doc.

example.title: migration-engine subagent prompt pattern
example.code: You are migration-engine running in dry-run mode. Inputs: source export (JSONL), field mapping (YAML), target schema (JSON). For each source record output a JSON object matching target schema with a `_source_id` field. Set `_validation_errors` array per record where mapping failed. Do not call any APIs. Do not invent fields.

example.title: cutover-runner subagent prompt pattern
example.code: You are cutover-runner. Input: checklist YAML with steps, owners, validation commands. Execute one step at a time. Wait for the command's exit code. If non-zero, halt and emit {step, command, exit_code, stdout, stderr} as JSON. Never skip steps. Never run two in parallel.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Reference tooling available for migration execution.

list.item: jira-cli / Linear GraphQL / gh / az devops — Source/target API drivers
parallel (GNU) — Batch ETL with controlled concurrency + retries. apt install parallel
flock — Single-writer cutover lock for shared state. coreutils builtin
restic / rclone — Backup source attachments before cutover. https://restic.net
dataset (Python) — SQLite staging for ID mapping + checkpoints. pip install dataset
httpx (Python) — Async HTTP client with retry/backoff for migration engine. pip install httpx[http2]
Unito (SaaS sync) — Two-way mirror during parallel run; useful for soft cutover. REST API.
Exalate (SaaS) — Programmable sync rules; good for partial scope migration. Groovy.
Atlassian Jira Cloud Migration Assistant (SaaS) — Server→Cloud only; not for cross-vendor.
Solidify Migrator (SaaS) — Azure DevOps ↔ Jira, supports CMMI; non-trivial cost.
Linear Importer (SaaS) — Good for one-shot, not for incremental waves. JSON upload.
Slack / Teams (notifications, SaaS) — Post each phase milestone for stakeholder transparency. Webhooks.
1Password / Vault (SaaS/OSS) — Store source + target API tokens; rotate post-cutover. CLI.
Migrate in waves by team or by project, not by record-type (so each team gets a complete-feeling tool, not a half-mirror).
Keep the source read-only for ≥30 days post-cutover; cheap insurance.
Cutover on Friday afternoon if the org tolerates a quiet weekend; never mid-week.
Pre-write the rollback comms — "we are reverting to <source> while we resolve <issue>" — so you can ship it in seconds.
Always validate three numbers (issues, comments, attachments-by-byte) and 50 random sampled items by hand before declaring success.
Decommission source on a calendar reminder, not on a vibe; teams will stay on it for years if you don't force the move.
Long-running migrations exceed agent token budgets; design the engine as a CLI tool the agent invokes, not as inline LLM calls.
LLMs love to "fix" mappings on the fly — explicitly forbid runtime mapping mutation; mapping is frozen at start.
A successful Phase 4 with 99.5% success looks fine, but the missing 0.5% is always the most-linked tickets. Force-list them.
Cutover checklist: agents mark steps complete based on stdout text matches, which lie. Use exit codes, not greps.
Comment / mention preservation needs user-ID resolution; agents that skip this strip @mentions silently.
Webhook-based comms can spam channels under retry storms; rate-limit notifications, prefer one summary per phase.
Token costs balloon if agents read raw issue bodies; pre-summarize to ≤200 tokens per record before reasoning.

template.purpose: Project plan template: scope, timeline, risk register, success criteria.
Checkpointed loader — resumes from .checkpoint on rerun; handles rate-limit backoff.
